author: @CCoburn3
text: Bamboo is good for demonstration purposes. But your initial idea of using fiberglass as the spring is better for any production system. Bamboo rots. That’s not something that you want when you have ten thousand generating units. Maintenance is an important factor in any design that is intended for long-term use.

author: @n9ne
text: Have you looked into The "Perpetual Motion" Machine. I am pretty convinced i figured out how it works. those 3 weights are placed so always 2 go down and 2 go up. then you have a tube that catches wind and on the bottom of the tube that catches wind is a housing that collects wind to build up pressure and then it shoots out the other end to keep the wheel spinning for 2 whole years apparently. those 2 weights turn int "3" weights vs 2 weights caused by wind inertia..i don't know the actual math so..

author: @jb-dn3ct
text: To be fair I think plastic printed ball and socket joints would probably be one of the better choices for build cost and the environment it needs to work in, if metal was used then grease would be needed to reduce wear and corrosion, plus the overall cost would be much higher do to the materials plus the milling/heating and freezing required to build this type of joint, wood would wear and rot so yes plastic is clearly the cheapest/simplest solution

author: @Enjoymentboy
text: Ok...I have to ask.  How in the world do you get the measurement boxes where you manually enter the dimensions to stay on the screen for so long???  This is my biggest gripe with tinkercad.  I click on the relevant box and the area opens that shows the dimension but it only stay on the screen for 2 or 3 seconds before it goes away on me.  It makes it incredibly annoying and aggravating to try and enter exact dimensions.  For instance if I want to make a box 30x40x20mm I can manually drag each edge out to where I want but it gets really fiddly trying to get it exact so I'd rather just type in the exact dimension.  When I select the relevant edge I see the box where I enter the number but before I can move my mouse to it to select it the box disappears.  Help???  lol

author: @jerrodlopes186
text:
Thank you, as always. Wonderful Ideas.

This gave me another one. I usually follow the philosophy of "simpler is better". Not always true, but in this case I think building a flap with a cylinder in the middle to fit over the spring shaft with the magnet on top situated within the coil greatly reduces the amount of arc swing thereby reducing voltage variances. This also reduces the amount of moving parts to the spring only. Only one possible point of failure. Think of a dandelion pouf in the wind.

Thoughts?

author: @justtinkering6713
text: I just realized a problem with this barely corn idea. If you have more than one of these they wouldn't all produce a positive polarity voltage at the same time, so if you wire them in series you would expect the voltages to add together.  Some would be producing a negative voltage. if they weren't in sync , they would null each other.  Current can't go both ways in a conductor at the same instant. Maybe each one could be connected to a capacitor, through a rectifier .

author: @generalawareness101
text: I used to 3d print then finally said enough is enough after a decade due to filament having to be kept dry.  Very big problem and once humidity gets at it even a dehydrator can't remove it all (that takes about 12-24h of running).  Ruins the quality.  I was about to go to resin (solving the humidity issue) to find the projection system is considered a consumable so after 50-500h (depending on various factors) you have to replace it, and they are not cheap (nor is the resin).

author: @yougeo
text:
Is it better to have a large magnet or rather a more powerful magnet moving through a coil slowly and a short distance or a small less powerful magnet moving through a coil either faster or for a longer distance to generate more power?

In other words is it better to have the magnet at the top of the hinge or a smaller magnet at the bottom of the hinge with the coil at the top or the bottom of the hinge?

replies.author: @JulienB
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering You did mention fields in the last episode. I must have misunderstood your intentions.

Though energy production density is still a valid concern. If each blade of "electronic grass" only produces a very small amount of energy, you'll need lots to make any significant impact.

So you either waste fields' worth of space, or end up not getting any significant power production out of it, even if you place them on otherwise "unused" surfaces.

And those surfaces aren't free either. The "land" cost still exists when you have to place the things on walls/roofs/signs, as you don't own those. So whether you pay someone to buy a field, or many someones to rent walls etc... you're still paying for the surface. And I seems to me that it would take a large surface per Watt.

Spreading those on very diverse surfaces also adds a cost for the power collection. At some point you'll need to wire them all to centralise the power somewhere, to use it, or to join to power grid.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
The ocean has very strong periodic up and down (and along a beach in many cases forward and back ) motions that would leverage vibrational movement generation far better than wind...
While I understand that you are considering the cost, another to add would be feasibility of "scaling"... and while this current invention is "low cost", it lacks the ability to scale because you would need a good 1000 of them just to make a few Amps at a ~2 volts. Taking that to the level of house current... you would need: probably 100 times as many just to reach house voltage... meaning you would need 100,000 units to generate 1 to 3 amp at house voltage.
Power = 2 volts * 0.003 A = .006 watts @ 2 volts. You would need 1000 units just to get to 6 watts at 2 volts., and 100 x 1000 units to get to 200 volts. (rough estimate).
